Unemployment improved to 16.2%. The economy was recovering well. However formal
employment remained low relative to OECD standards. Informal sector was large.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2014.assessment:
Unemployment rose slightly to 17.6% as economic growth moderated. The Russian
economic crisis in late 2014 began to reduce remittances and trade, affecting
employment. Emigration to Russia and elsewhere continued.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2015.assessment:
Unemployment rose to 18.5% as the Russian economic crisis fully transmitted.
Remittances from Russia fell significantly. The Electric Yerevan protests
reflected economic discontent with unemployment and cost of living. Growth slowed.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2016.assessment:
Unemployment remained elevated at 18.0% during the near-stagnation year (0.2%
real growth). The NK April war added economic uncertainty. Youth unemployment
was particularly high. Emigration continued as an employment pressure valve.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2017.assessment:
Unemployment improved marginally to 17.8% alongside strong 7.5% economic growth.
However the improvement was modest given the growth rate. Services and tourism grew
but did not substantially reduce unemployment. Pre-revolution discontent about
unemployment contributed to the 2018 mobilization.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2018.assessment:
Unemployment remained high at 17.7% in the Velvet Revolution year. The revolution
itself was partly driven by economic grievances including unemployment. Post-revolution
business confidence improved, expected to eventually create jobs.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2019.assessment:
Unemployment remained stubbornly at 17.7% despite 7.6% GDP growth. The post-revolution
Pashinyan government was struggling to translate strong macro growth into employment
gains. Tourism and IT sectors grew but were insufficient to reduce structural unemployment.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2020.assessment:
Unemployment rose to 19.0% due to the COVID-19 pandemic and the NK war. Tourism
collapsed; services employment fell; war-related disruption affected the economy.
The dual crisis increased unemployment significantly.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2021.assessment:
Unemployment improved to 16.5% as the economy recovered from the dual 2020 shocks.
Services and tourism began recovering; IT sector grew. Recovery was faster than expected.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2022.assessment:
Unemployment fell significantly to around 13% as the influx of Russian emigrants
(estimated 100,000+) brought new economic activity and IT employment. Russian IT
companies relocated to Armenia; new businesses were created; demand for local services
surged. This was the lowest unemployment in years.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, SL.UEM.TOTL.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2023.assessment:
Unemployment continued to be affected by the Russian emigrant presence and the
NK refugee influx (100,000 Armenians from NK). The NK refugee integration created
additional demand for labor. IT sector remained strong.

sources.citation: IMF Armenia Article IV, 2023

2024.assessment:
Unemployment expected to remain in the 12-15% range. IT sector continued as
key employer. Russian emigrant presence moderating but structural changes in
the labor market from 2022-2023 were partially permanent.

sources.citation: IMF World Economic Outlook projections, Armenia, 2024

2025.assessment:
Unemployment expected to remain elevated but gradually improving with EU integration
trajectory and continued IT sector development.

2005.assessment:
Armenia maintained normal diplomatic relations internationally. Member of UN,
Council of Europe, OSCE, CIS. EU Partnership and Cooperation Agreement in place.
The unresolved NK conflict created tensions with Azerbaijan and Turkey but no
broader isolation. Strong diaspora ties with France, Russia, and the US.

sources.citation: Council of Europe, Armenia membership record, 2005

2006.assessment:
Normal diplomatic integration maintained. EU European Neighbourhood Policy
Action Plan was being implemented. Russia remained the primary strategic partner.

sources.citation: European Commission, ENP Progress Report Armenia, 2006

2007.assessment:
Normal diplomatic integration. The OSCE Minsk Group continued NK mediation.
Armenia participated actively in Council of Europe and international organizations.

sources.citation: OSCE Annual Report, 2007

2008.assessment:
Post-election violence drew Council of Europe and EU criticism but no diplomatic
rupture. Armenia maintained full diplomatic integration. The Armenian-Turkish
normalization process was advancing.

sources.citation: Council of Europe, Armenia monitoring report, 2008

2009.assessment:
Armenia-Turkey protocols were signed in October 2009, representing a diplomatic
breakthrough attempt. If ratified, they would have opened the closed Turkey-Armenia
border and established diplomatic relations. However the protocols were ultimately
not ratified. Normal integration with all other countries maintained.

sources.citation: Reuters, Armenia-Turkey protocols signed, October 2009

2010.assessment:
Armenia-Turkey normalization stalled. EU Eastern Partnership launched 2009. Normal
diplomatic relations with all parties maintained.

sources.citation: EU Eastern Partnership, Armenia, 2010

2011.assessment:
Normal diplomatic integration. EU Association Agreement and DCFTA negotiations
began. Armenia participated in the EU Eastern Partnership Warsaw Summit.

sources.citation: EU Eastern Partnership Warsaw Summit, 2011

2012.assessment:
Armenia continued on the EU Association Agreement track. Normal relations with
Russia and international organizations. OSCE Minsk Group maintained NK mediation.

sources.citation: European Commission, ENP Progress Report Armenia, 2012

2013.assessment:
Armenia's September 2013 decision to abandon EU AA/DCFTA and join EAEU caused
significant diplomatic friction with EU but no breakdown. Armenia remained in
Eastern Partnership framework. Relations with Russia strengthened as strategic partner.

sources.citation: Reuters, Armenia to join Russia customs union, September 2013

2014.assessment:
Armenia joined EAEU (January 2015). EU relations continued through new CEPA
track. Normal relations with all major powers. Russia primary security partner.

sources.citation: European Commission, Armenia CEPA negotiations beginning, 2014

2015.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration as EAEU member and Council of Europe/OSCE participant.
CEPA negotiations with EU ongoing. 100th anniversary of Armenian Genocide drew
major international attention with 60+ heads of state visiting.

sources.citation: European Commission, Armenia CEPA progress, 2015

2016.assessment:
Normal diplomatic integration. April NK war drew international mediation effort.
CEPA negotiations with EU continued. Armenia maintained balanced diplomacy between
Russia (EAEU/CSTO) and West.

sources.citation: EU EEAS, Statement on Nagorno-Karabakh April 2016

2017.assessment:
CEPA with EU signed in November 2017 at Eastern Partnership Summit — a major
milestone. Armenia maintained EAEU membership while deepening EU ties.
Full diplomatic integration.

sources.citation: European Commission, CEPA signed November 2017

2018.assessment:
The Velvet Revolution significantly improved Armenia's international standing.
Western governments celebrated the democratic transition. CEPA provisional
application enhanced EU ties. Strong diplomatic integration across all major
partners. Russia maintained strategic ties despite political change.

sources.citation: EU EEAS, Statement on Armenia Velvet Revolution, May 2018

2019.assessment:
Armenia maintained strong diplomatic integration. EU relations deepened under CEPA.
US relations positive; multiple US Secretaries of State and VP visits. Russia
maintained strategic partnership. Multi-vector diplomacy working effectively.

sources.citation: European Commission, CEPA implementation, 2019

2020.assessment:
The NK war complicated Armenia's diplomatic integration. Russia brokered ceasefire
but Armenia questioned Russian support adequacy. Western states called for ceasefire.
Post-war Armenia-Russia relations strained. EU and US expressed solidarity with Armenia.

sources.citation: Reuters, International responses to NK ceasefire, November 2020

2021.assessment:
Armenia maintained full diplomatic relations. Post-war diplomatic reorientation
began gradually toward EU. EU civil observation mission (EUMA) deployed to
Armenia-Azerbaijan border. Russia remained a partner but trust was damaged.

sources.citation: EU EEAS, EUMA Armenia, 2021-2022

2022.assessment:
Armenia's diplomatic pivot to EU accelerated markedly. September Azerbaijani
offensive on Armenia proper drew EU and US diplomatic response. Russia's limited
reaction accelerated Armenia's western turn. EUMA expanded. EU-mediated peace
talks in Brussels and Prague.

sources.citation: EU External Action Service, EUMA expansion, 2022

2023.assessment:
Armenia's diplomatic realignment accelerated. CSTO participation suspended after
NK offensive and lack of CSTO response. New partnership agreements with EU signed.
US security assistance increased. Armenia formally aligned with democratic West
while maintaining formal EAEU membership under duress.

sources.citation: Reuters, Armenia suspends CSTO activities, 2023
EU EEAS, Armenia-EU partnership agreements, 2023

2024.assessment:
Armenia deepened EU partnership. Visa liberalization discussions advanced.
New EU partnership agreement negotiations. Armenia applied for EU candidate status.
Russia ties continued cooling. Western diplomatic integration was at its strongest
in Armenia's post-Soviet history.

sources.citation: EU EEAS, Armenia EU candidate status, 2024

2025.assessment:
Armenia continued EU integration trajectory. Peace treaty with Azerbaijan
negotiations ongoing. Western diplomatic integration at historic high.

2005.assessment:
FDI inflows were approximately 3.4% of GDP in 2005, reflecting moderate investor
confidence. Russia was the largest FDI source, primarily in energy (Gazprom acquired
gas distribution) and telecom. The construction boom attracted some foreign investment.
The NK conflict and closed Turkey border were deterrents to broader FDI.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2006.assessment:
FDI surged to 6.6% of GDP in 2006 as construction and real estate attracted
major Russian and diaspora Armenian investment. The boom economy was drawing
capital. Oligarchic interests dominated FDI.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2007.assessment:
FDI peaked at 11.3% of GDP in 2007 — extraordinarily high. This reflected the
peak of the construction and real estate boom with major Russian oligarchic
investment. The economy was attracting significant capital driven by high returns
in the construction sector.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2008.assessment:
FDI remained high at 8.6% of GDP but began declining from the 2007 peak.
The global financial crisis in H2 2008 was causing capital outflows globally.
Armenia's political crisis (March election) also created uncertainty.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2009.assessment:
FDI fell to 6.2% of GDP but remained elevated given the economic crisis. The
collapse of the construction sector reduced real estate FDI. Some energy sector
Russian investment continued.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2010.assessment:
FDI continued to decline to 4.4% of GDP. Post-crisis investment climate was
cautious. Russia remained the dominant investor. Mining sector attracted some FDI.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2011.assessment:
FDI stabilized at 4.9% of GDP. Mining sector attracted continued investment.
Russia, France, and Germany were main investor countries. Post-crisis recovery
improving investor sentiment.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2012.assessment:
FDI maintained at 4.9% of GDP. Armenia's strong 7.2% growth attracted continued
investment. The oligarchic business environment remained a deterrent for non-Russian
investors while familiar CIS investors continued.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2013.assessment:
FDI declined to 3.4% of GDP. The September 2013 abandonment of the EU AA/DCFTA
track in favor of EAEU reduced Western FDI attractiveness. EAEU alignment boosted
Russian investment prospects.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2014.assessment:
FDI at 3.3% of GDP was declining as Russian capital flight from sanctions
concerns and rouble crisis affected investment capacity. EAEU accession in 2015
was expected to boost CIS FDI.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2015.assessment:
FDI fell to 1.9% of GDP as the Russian economic crisis significantly reduced
Russian investment in Armenia. EAEU membership had not yet produced the expected
boost. Near-stagnation and Electric Yerevan protests added uncertainty.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2016.assessment:
FDI remained low at 1.6% of GDP in the near-stagnation year. NK April war added
security risk. Mining sector continued to attract some FDI. Russia was recovering
but still not investing at pre-crisis rates.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2017.assessment:
FDI recovered to 2.6% of GDP alongside the economic rebound. Russia and diaspora
investment recovered. The CEPA with EU signature attracted interest from EU investors.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2018.assessment:
FDI at 2.4% of GDP was maintained. The Velvet Revolution created excitement about
anti-corruption and democratic governance improvements, which was expected to
boost FDI in subsequent years. Post-revolution business confidence improved.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2019.assessment:
FDI was 1.8% of GDP despite strong 7.6% growth. The anti-corruption prosecutions
of former officials (including Kocharyan) created some uncertainty for established
oligarchic investors while the prospect of improved rule of law was expected to
attract new investors. Tourism and IT investment grew.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2020.assessment:
FDI fell to 1.5% of GDP as COVID-19 and the NK war created dual shocks. Global FDI
declined sharply in 2020. NK war increased security risk for investors.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2021.assessment:
FDI remained low at 1.5% of GDP as post-war uncertainty persisted. IT sector
attracted some investment. Post-COVID recovery attracted some services investment.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2022.assessment:
FDI surged to approximately 4.5% of GDP driven by massive relocation of Russian
IT companies and capital following Western sanctions on Russia. Russian companies
registered in Armenia to maintain access to international financial systems.
This was a structural change to Armenia's investment landscape.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, BX.KLT.DINV.WD.GD.ZS, Armenia, accessed 2026-03-01

2023.assessment:
FDI remained elevated as Russian IT companies stayed in Armenia and EU interest
grew. The NK refugee influx also brought some capital. EU integration prospects
attracted new EU investors. FDI composition was shifting from Russia-dominated
to more diverse sources.

sources.citation: IMF Armenia Article IV, 2023

2024.assessment:
FDI expected to remain moderate. EU integration prospects expected to attract
European investors. IT sector remained a strong attractor.

2025.assessment:
FDI expected to remain moderate. EU integration trajectory expected to diversify
FDI sources away from Russia-dominant toward EU-dominant.

2005.assessment:
Russia maintained a military base at Gyumri (102nd Russian Military Base) under
bilateral agreement. This was a significant invited Russian military presence.
Additionally Russian border troops were present on some sections of Armenia's
borders. This was invited presence consistent with CSTO membership.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2005, Russian forces in Armenia

2006.assessment:
Russian military presence (102nd base at Gyumri) continued under bilateral
agreement. CSTO collective security framework. No uninvited foreign military.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2006, Russian forces in Armenia

2007.assessment:
Russian 102nd Military Base at Gyumri continued operations. CSTO collective
defense framework active. Armenia maintained sovereignty over its territory
with invited Russian presence.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2007

2008.assessment:
Russian 102nd Military Base continued at Gyumri. The August 2008 Russia-Georgia
war affected the regional security context but did not change Armenia's foreign
military presence. Armenia did not participate in the Russia-Georgia conflict.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2008

2009.assessment:
Russian 102nd Military Base at Gyumri continued under 2010 extended agreement
(base lease extended to 2044). CSTO collective security arrangements active.

sources.citation: Russia-Armenia base agreement, extended 2010

2010.assessment:
Russia-Armenia military base agreement extended to 2044. Russian 102nd base
at Gyumri was significantly expanded and upgraded. CSTO membership continued.
Russia supplied advanced weapons to Armenia.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2010; Russia-Armenia base extension agreement

2011.assessment:
Russian 102nd Military Base at Gyumri maintained with approximately 5,000 troops.
Russian border guards present on some borders. CSTO membership active.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2011

2012.assessment:
Russian 102nd Military Base continued. CSTO collective security framework active.
No significant changes to foreign military presence.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2012

2013.assessment:
Russian 102nd base ongoing. Armenia joined EAEU reinforcing economic-political
alignment with Russia alongside the military CSTO framework.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2013

2014.assessment:
Russian 102nd Military Base at Gyumri continued. CSTO active. Russia provided
advanced weapons including Iskander missiles to Armenia. The relationship was
Armenia's primary security guarantee against Azerbaijan.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2014

2015.assessment:
Russian 102nd Military Base continued as primary foreign military presence.
CSTO membership active. Russian arms supplies continued. No other significant
foreign military presence in Armenia proper.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2015

2016.assessment:
Russian 102nd Military Base continued. The April NK war demonstrated limits of
Russian protection — CSTO did not invoke collective defense for NK. Russian forces
remained in Armenia proper at Gyumri. CSTO membership continued.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2016

2017.assessment:
Russian 102nd base ongoing. CSTO participation continued. Russia remained the
dominant security partner. No significant changes to foreign military presence.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2017

2018.assessment:
Russian 102nd Military Base continued under the existing agreement. The Velvet
Revolution did not alter the base agreement. Pashinyan government maintained
CSTO membership and the Russian base arrangement while simultaneously seeking
closer EU ties.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2018

2019.assessment:
Russian 102nd base continued. Armenia maintained CSTO membership. Pashinyan
government maintained balance between Russia (security) and EU/US (diplomacy/democracy).

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2019

2020.assessment:
Russian 102nd base at Gyumri continued. NK war revealed limits of Russian and
CSTO security guarantees — neither intervened militarily in the NK conflict itself
(which involved sovereign Azerbaijan territory). Post-war, Russian peacekeepers
(~2,000) were deployed to NK under the November 2020 ceasefire.

sources.citation: Russia MoD, Russian peacekeepers deployed to NK, November 2020

2021.assessment:
Russian 102nd base at Gyumri continued. Russian peacekeepers maintained in NK.
The May 2021 Azerbaijani incursion into Armenian sovereign territory highlighted
CSTO inadequacy — no CSTO response. EU civil observation mission (EUMA) was
being discussed. This planted seeds for Armenia's security reorientation.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2021

2022.assessment:
The EU Monitoring Mission (EUMA) deployed to the Armenia-Azerbaijan border in
October 2022 — a significant Western security presence in Armenia. Russian 102nd
base continued. September 2022 Azerbaijani offensive into Armenia proper showed
CSTO still would not respond. EU presence growing alongside existing Russian base.

sources.citation: EU EEAS, EUMA deployment October 2022

2023.assessment:
Armenia's security arrangements shifted significantly. EUMA presence on border.
Russian peacekeepers withdrew from NK after September 2023 Azerbaijani offensive.
Armenia suspended CSTO participation. Russian 102nd base remained under the 2044
agreement but was diplomatically contested. Armenia requested US and EU security
assistance.

sources.citation: Reuters, Armenia suspends CSTO, 2023
EU EEAS, EUMA Armenia 2023

2024.assessment:
Armenia continued to rebalance security arrangements toward EU/US. EUMA maintained.
Armenia sought to negotiate terms for Russian base withdrawal or reduction.
Joint military exercises with EU and US began. The transition from Russian to
Western security orientation was advanced but not yet complete.

sources.citation: News reports, Armenia security reorientation, 2024

2025.assessment:
Armenia's security transition toward EU/US framework was ongoing. Status of
Russian base remained under negotiation.

economic emigrants rather than refugees. Some Armenians received asylum in
Western countries but not as recognized refugees from active persecution. The
NK conflict had produced its major displacement in 1991-1994; the situation
had stabilized.

Minimal refugee flows. Economic emigration to Russia and EU countries continued

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Armenia, 2006

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Armenia, 2007

applications in Western countries. However, overall refugee flows remained
minimal. The UNHCR did not register significant new outflows.

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Armenia, 2008

Minimal refugee flows. The economic crisis of 2009 may have driven more economic

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Armenia, 2013

Minimal refugee flows. Stable domestic situation. Some Syrian-Armenians returned

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Armenia, 2014

2015.assessment:
Minimal refugee flows from Armenia. Economic emigration continued. Syrian-Armenian

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Armenia, 2015

2016.assessment:
The April NK four-day war may have caused limited displacement in the NK conflict

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Armenia, 2016

2017.assessment:
Minimal refugee flows. Stable pre-revolution period. Economic emigration

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Armenia, 2017

Minimal refugee flows. The Velvet Revolution was peaceful with no forced

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Armenia, 2018

Minimal refugee flows. Post-revolution political stability maintained.

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Armenia, 2019

2020.assessment:
The 44-day NK war (September-November 2020) caused significant displacement.
Tens of thousands of NK Armenians fled to Armenia during the war. Many returned
after the ceasefire. An estimated 30,000-40,000 may have remained displaced
at year end. This represents approximately 1% of Armenia's population.
Armenian proper saw minimal direct displacement.

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, NK/Armenia displacement, 2020

2021.assessment:
Post-war displacement partially resolved. Many NK Armenians who had fled returned

sources.citation: UNHCR Refugee Data Finder, Armenia, 2021

2022.assessment:
The September 2022 Azerbaijani offensive on Armenian sovereign territory caused
some internal displacement. Additionally, some NK Armenians who had returned
were displaced again. Total new displacement estimated at several tens of thousands.

sources.citation: IDMC, Internal Displacement Armenia, 2022

2023.assessment:
The September 2023 Azerbaijani offensive in Nagorno-Karabakh caused catastrophic
displacement. Approximately 100,000 ethnic Armenians fled from NK to Armenia within

million population as sudden new arrivals. The Armenian government and international
organizations mounted a humanitarian response.

New displacement from Armenia itself remained minimal in 2024. The NK refugees

sender of significant displacement flows.

sources.citation: UNHCR Armenia Country Reports, 2024

2025.assessment:
Armenia expected to have minimal new displacement. NK refugees continuing
integration. Stable domestic situation.
