No international sanctions on Armenia. After the February 2022 Ukraine invasion,
Armenia became a significant parallel import hub circumventing Western sanctions on
Russia. Western governments expressed concern but did not sanction Armenia.
Armenia's domestic political pivot away from Russia reduced sanctioning motivation.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC, Sanctions Programs, 2022

2023.assessment:
No international sanctions on Armenia. Western pressure on parallel imports
intensified but Armenia was undergoing a diplomatic shift toward the EU and West.
Some US/EU secondary sanctions warnings were issued to Armenian companies
involved in Russia sanctions circumvention.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC, Sanctions Programs, 2023

2024.assessment:
No country-level sanctions on Armenia. Some individual Armenian companies faced
US secondary sanctions for Russia trade. Armenia's overall diplomatic positioning
toward the West reduced sanctions risk.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC, Sanctions Programs, 2024

2025.assessment:
No sanctions on Armenia expected given the EU integration trajectory and Western
diplomatic support for Armenian democracy.

2005.assessment:
Armenia received a Civil Liberties score of 4 (Partly Free) from Freedom House in
2005. Media freedom was restricted with pro-government bias; freedom of assembly
was constrained; the 2004 constitutional referendum was held under pressure. The
Kocharian government maintained authoritarian tendencies while holding elections.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006, Civil Liberties Score for Armenia

2006.assessment:
Armenia maintained its Partly Free status with Civil Liberties score of 4. Media
remained largely controlled by pro-government interests; civic space was limited;
religious minorities faced some discrimination. The 2007 constitutional amendments
were passed but civil society remained constrained.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007, Civil Liberties Score for Armenia

2007.assessment:
Civil liberties remained constrained. Parliamentary elections in May 2007 were
marked by irregularities. Freedom of press was limited with broadcast media
dominated by pro-government stations. Opposition faced harassment and restrictions
on assembly.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008, Civil Liberties Score for Armenia

2008.assessment:
Civil liberties significantly deteriorated following the disputed February 2008
presidential election. A 20-day state of emergency was declared after security
forces killed approximately 10 protesters. Media was restricted; opposition leaders
were arrested; assembly was banned. Armenia's worst post-independence crackdown.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009, Civil Liberties Score for Armenia
HRW, 'Armenia: Post-Election Violence', 2008

2009.assessment:
Civil liberties partially recovered from the 2008 crackdown as the state of emergency
was lifted and some opposition figures released. Media freedom remained restricted.
The government pursued Armenian-Turkish normalization which somewhat improved
international standing but domestic civil space remained constrained.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010, Civil Liberties Score for Armenia

2010.assessment:
Armenia maintained its Partly Free status with constrained civil liberties.
Broadcast media remained under government influence; online media grew as an
alternative. Civil society organizations operated but faced bureaucratic obstacles.
Freedom of religion faced some restrictions particularly for minority groups.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011, Civil Liberties Score for Armenia

2011.assessment:
Civil liberties remained partly constrained. Protests against the government occurred
and were permitted but with harassment. Media pluralism was limited; print media
offered more diversity than broadcast. Opposition parties operated but faced difficulties.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012, Civil Liberties Score for Armenia

2012.assessment:
Parliamentary elections in May 2012 proceeded with irregularities. Civil liberties
score remained at 4. Press freedom was limited. The constitutional reform process
aimed at shifting Armenia to a parliamentary system began.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013, Civil Liberties Score for Armenia

2013.assessment:
Civil liberties score remained at 4 (Partly Free). Presidential elections in February
2013 saw Sargsyan re-elected in first round with opposition alleging fraud. Internet
freedom was mostly maintained. Civil society grew but faced government pressure.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2014, Civil Liberties Score for Armenia

2014.assessment:
Armenia maintained Partly Free status. Press freedom faced new challenges as
internet media faced increased pressure. The oligarchic system controlled much of
the economy and media. Civil society remained active but constrained.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015, Civil Liberties Score for Armenia

2015.assessment:
The Electric Yerevan protests in June 2015 over electricity price increases were
large and largely peaceful but saw police use of water cannons and detentions.
Civil liberties score remained at 4. The controversial constitutional referendum
in December 2015 (shifting to parliamentary system) was criticized as rigged.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2016, Civil Liberties Score for Armenia

2016.assessment:
Civil liberties remained constrained. The Daredevils of Sassoun armed group seizure
of a Yerevan police station in July 2016 and the government crackdown produced
solidarity protests that were dispersed. Media freedom remained limited. Score 4.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2017, Civil Liberties Score for Armenia

2017.assessment:
Civil liberties remained constrained under the Sargsyan government. Parliamentary
elections in April 2017 were held under new proportional system but with irregularities.
Media freedom was limited; oligarchic control of broadcast media persisted. Growing
civic activism was evident in the lead-up to the 2018 revolution.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2018, Civil Liberties Score for Armenia

2018.assessment:
The Velvet Revolution significantly improved civil liberties in Armenia. Freedom House
upgraded Armenia's Civil Liberties score from 4 to 3 following the peaceful revolution
and Pashinyan's election as prime minister. Media freedom improved; political prisoners
from the 2008 crackdown were released; assembly was freely permitted.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2019, Civil Liberties Score for Armenia
Carnegie Endowment, 'Armenia's Velvet Revolution', 2018

2019.assessment:
Armenia maintained improved civil liberties under Pashinyan's government. Media
freedom improved with reduced oligarchic control; assembly rights were respected;
political prisoners from the 2008 crackdown had been released. However, some concerns
about prosecutions of former officials being politically motivated emerged.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2020, Civil Liberties Score for Armenia

2020.assessment:
Civil liberties were affected by the 2020 Nagorno-Karabakh War. Martial law was
declared during the war (September-November), restricting some freedoms temporarily.
Post-war protests against Pashinyan over the ceasefire terms led to tensions.
Civil liberties score remained at 3 but under pressure from war crisis.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2021, Civil Liberties Score for Armenia

2021.assessment:
Civil liberties remained at score 3. Early parliamentary elections in June 2021
saw Pashinyan win despite opposition demands for his resignation over the NK war.
Freedom of assembly and expression were largely maintained. Media pluralism improved.
Some concerns about politicized prosecutions continued.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022, Civil Liberties Score for Armenia

2022.assessment:
Armenia maintained Civil Liberties score of 3. The September 2022 Azerbaijani
offensive into Armenian territory created a national security crisis but civil
liberties in Armenia proper were maintained. Media pluralism continued to improve.
Rule of law improved gradually; some judicial independence concerns remained.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023, Civil Liberties Score for Armenia

2023.assessment:
Armenia maintained Civil Liberties score of 3. The September 2023 Azerbaijani
offensive in NK resolved the NK conflict through displacement of the Armenian population
(~100,000 refugees to Armenia). The government managed the refugee crisis. Freedom
of media, assembly, and expression were maintained. Continuing move toward EU integration.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2024, Civil Liberties Score for Armenia

2024.assessment:
Armenia continued to maintain its Civil Liberties score around 3. EU integration
trajectory supported continued civil society development. Media freedom and assembly
rights were broadly respected. Judicial independence remained a concern but improving.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025, Civil Liberties Score for Armenia

2025.assessment:
Armenia continued its democratic trajectory under Pashinyan with civil liberties
broadly maintained. EU visa liberalization discussions and partnership agreement
negotiations supported civil society development.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2026, Civil Liberties Score for Armenia (expected)

2005.assessment:
Under President Kocharian, Armenia's ruling coalition was relatively stable. The
Republican Party of Armenia (RPA) and allied oligarchic interests formed a cohesive
bloc. The Orinats Yerkir and ARFD (Dashnaktsutyun) parties were partner coalition
members. Elite cohesion was maintained through patronage networks and shared
economic interests.

sources.citation: Carnegie Endowment, 'Armenia's Political Transition', 2005

2006.assessment:
Elite cohesion remained stable. Preparations for the succession from Kocharian to
Sargsyan were underway with limited intra-elite conflict. The oligarchic network
surrounding the ruling coalition remained unified.

sources.citation: ICG, 'Armenia: Picking up the Pieces', 2006

2007.assessment:
Elite cohesion was maintained ahead of the 2008 presidential election. Sargsyan
was being positioned as Kocharian's successor. Some elite competition existed but
was managed within the ruling network. Parliamentary elections saw RPA consolidate
power.

sources.citation: Carnegie Endowment, Armenia political analysis, 2007

2008.assessment:
The February 2008 disputed presidential election and subsequent crackdown created
significant elite tensions. Former President Ter-Petrossian emerged as opposition
leader mobilizing mass protests. The military and security services remained loyal
to Sargsyan, allowing the crackdown. Elite split between ruling coalition and
opposition crystallized.

sources.citation: HRW, 'Armenia: Post-Election Violence', 2008

2009.assessment:
Elite cohesion partially restored after the 2008 crisis. Sargsyan consolidated
power. Some former coalition partners (ARFD) left the coalition but this was managed.
The ruling network remained unified around the RPA with oligarchic support.

sources.citation: Carnegie Endowment, Armenia political analysis, 2009

2010.assessment:
Ruling elite cohesion was stable. Sargsyan's RPA dominated with oligarchic
backing. The opposition remained fragmented. No significant intra-elite splits
or coup threats. Patronage networks kept key stakeholders aligned.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Nations in Transit 2011, Armenia

2011.assessment:
Elite cohesion remained stable. Key oligarchs and regional power brokers stayed
aligned with the Sargsyan government. Growing economic discontent was not yet
translating into elite defections. The opposition alliance around Ter-Petrossian
was weakening.

sources.citation: Carnegie Endowment, Armenia political analysis, 2011

2012.assessment:
Parliamentary elections in May 2012 saw RPA maintain dominance. Elite cohesion
was maintained. Some oligarchs adjusted positions around the new parliament but
the core ruling coalition held. The Prosperous Armenia Party (PAP) competed but
within managed boundaries.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Nations in Transit 2013, Armenia

2013.assessment:
Presidential elections confirmed Sargsyan's dominance. Elite remained cohesive
around the ruling network. The shift toward patronal presidentialism under Sargsyan
was consolidated. Gagik Tsarukyan's Prosperous Armenia maintained elite competition
but within manageable parameters.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Nations in Transit 2014, Armenia

2014.assessment:
In early 2015, oligarch Tsarukyan of Prosperous Armenia was forced to resign his
parliamentary position and briefly leave politics after confrontation with Sargsyan.
This reflected tensions within the elite network but ultimately demonstrated
Sargsyan's ability to suppress intra-elite competition, not weakness.

sources.citation: Carnegie Endowment, 'Armenia at the Brink?', 2014

2015.assessment:
The Electric Yerevan protests revealed elite anxiety about public discontent but
the government managed the crisis. The constitutional referendum was pushed through
over opposition protests. Elite cohesion around the plan to extend Sargsyan's rule
through a parliamentary system shift was maintained.

sources.citation: ICG, 'Armenia's Velvet Revolution Revisited', 2018 (retrospective)

2016.assessment:
Elite cohesion showed stress points. The April NK war and July Daredevils hostage
crisis tested the government. Parliamentary elections in April 2017 were approaching.
RPA maintained control but growing civil society mobilization signaled elite
vulnerability. Oligarchic networks remained aligned with the government.

sources.citation: Carnegie Endowment, Armenia political analysis, 2016

2017.assessment:
Parliamentary elections in April 2017 saw RPA win but with growing opposition.
Elite cohesion remained intact around Sargsyan and the oligarchic network. However,
growing civil society mobilization by Nikol Pashinyan's Yelk alliance was beginning
to attract some elite defectors. The ruling network faced increasing legitimacy challenges.

sources.citation: Carnegie Endowment, Armenia pre-revolution analysis, 2017

2018.assessment:
The Velvet Revolution (April-May 2018) shattered the ruling elite's cohesion. Mass
protests forced Sargsyan to resign as prime minister after only 3 days. Key political
elites defected or stepped aside; the military and police did not crack down
decisively. The RPA cooperated with Pashinyan's election in parliament. The old
oligarchic-political network was fragmented.

sources.citation: ICG, 'After Armenia's Revolution', September 2018
Carnegie Endowment, 'Armenia's Velvet Revolution', 2018

2019.assessment:
Post-revolution, a new elite configuration emerged around Pashinyan's Civil Contract
party. Former ruling elites (RPA, oligarchs) were weakened, facing prosecution. The
new governing elite was internally cohesive given the revolutionary mandate but faced
challenges in consolidating governance capacity. Former President Kocharian was arrested.

sources.citation: ICG, 'After Armenia's Revolution', 2018-2019

2020.assessment:
The 2020 Nagorno-Karabakh War caused severe elite fragmentation. Post-war, opposition
leaders including former presidents Kocharyan and Sargsyan united against Pashinyan.
Military leadership tensions arose, with some generals publicly opposing the ceasefire.
Street protests demanded Pashinyan's resignation. Pashinyan survived but elite cohesion
in the new governing coalition was severely tested.

sources.citation: ICG, 'Averting a New Confrontation in Nagorno-Karabakh', November 2020

2021.assessment:
The June 2021 snap elections resolved the immediate political crisis with Pashinyan
winning ~54% of the vote. The former regime elite (RPA, Kocharyan) competed but
lost decisively. Elite cohesion around the Pashinyan government improved after
the election result confirmed popular mandate. Some former opposition figures
accepted the democratic outcome.

sources.citation: OSCE/ODIHR, Final Report on June 2021 Parliamentary Elections

2022.assessment:
Elite cohesion improved under the Civil Contract government. The September 2022
Azerbaijani offensive on Armenia proper created a national crisis that actually
rallied domestic elites around the government. Pashinyan's pivot to EU integration
was contested by some pro-Russian voices but the governing elite remained unified.

sources.citation: Carnegie Endowment, Armenia political analysis, 2022

2023.assessment:
Elite cohesion was largely maintained. The September 2023 NK offensive and mass
exodus of Armenians from NK to Armenia were managed without major intra-elite
conflict. The EU partnership trajectory had broad elite support. Opposition remained
in parliament but accepted the democratic process.

sources.citation: Carnegie Endowment, Armenia political analysis, 2023

2024.assessment:
Armenia's governing elite remained cohesive around the Civil Contract party and
Pashinyan. EU integration talks and peace negotiations with Azerbaijan provided
shared policy goals. Opposition in parliament was vocal but not destabilizing.

sources.citation: News reports, Armenia political situation, 2024

2025.assessment:
Armenia maintained stable elite configuration. Peace process with Azerbaijan continued.
EU integration was the dominant policy trajectory with broad elite support.

sources.citation: News reports, Armenia political situation, 2025

2005.assessment:
Armenia's institutions functioned formally but with significant authoritarian
tendencies under President Kocharian. Courts were not independent; elections were
marred by fraud and intimidation; the legislature was dominated by pro-government
forces. The 2004 constitutional referendum was held under international criticism.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Armenia, 2005. Coppedge et al. (2024)
Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006, Armenia

2006.assessment:
Institutions functioned in a constrained manner. Executive dominated the legislature
and judiciary. Elections were not considered free and fair by international observers.
Civil society operated but with limited influence on government.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Armenia, 2006. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2007.assessment:
Parliamentary elections in May 2007 maintained the ruling party's dominance.
OSCE observers noted significant irregularities. Judiciary lacked independence.
Legislature functioned but as rubber stamp for executive decisions.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Armenia, 2007. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2008.assessment:
Institutional functioning deteriorated sharply after the February 2008 disputed
presidential election. State of emergency was declared; opposition leaders arrested;
courts upheld fraudulent election results; legislature rubber-stamped emergency
measures. The worst democratic regression since independence.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Armenia, 2008. Coppedge et al. (2024)
HRW, 'Armenia: Investigate Post-Election Violence', 2008
V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Armenia, 2009. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2010.assessment:
Institutions functioned in a constrained but mostly stable manner. Judiciary
remained dependent on executive. Legislature had nominal role. Municipal elections
showed some improvement but significant irregularities persisted.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Armenia, 2010. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2011.assessment:
Institutional functioning remained at competitive authoritarian levels. Opposition
was allowed to operate and hold protests but faced harassment. Courts showed
minimal independence. Legislature was dominated by Republican Party of Armenia.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Armenia, 2011. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2012.assessment:
Parliamentary elections in May 2012 showed some improvement over prior elections
per OSCE observers, though significant issues remained. The Republican Party of
Armenia maintained dominance. Institutions remained formally functional but
lacking genuine independence.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Armenia, 2012. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2013.assessment:
Presidential elections in February 2013 saw Sargsyan re-elected in the first round
with opposition alleging fraud. OSCE noted improvements over 2008 but substantive
concerns remained. Institutions functioned within the competitive authoritarian model.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Armenia, 2013. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2014.assessment:
Institutions continued to function within competitive authoritarian constraints.
Constitutional reform discussions began, aimed at shifting Armenia to a parliamentary
system. Some NGOs noted marginal improvements in judicial transparency.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Armenia, 2014. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2015.assessment:
The December 2015 constitutional referendum approved the shift to a parliamentary
system, widely seen as Sargsyan's way to extend his rule beyond term limits. The
referendum was criticized by opposition and international observers for procedural
irregularities. Institutions remained in competitive authoritarian mode.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Armenia, 2015. Coppedge et al. (2024)
Venice Commission, Opinion on Constitutional Referendum, 2015

2016.assessment:
Institutions functioned at competitive authoritarian baseline. Parliamentary
elections in April 2017 were approaching, generating political competition.
The Republican Party of Armenia maintained dominance. Judiciary remained dependent.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Armenia, 2016. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2017.assessment:
Parliamentary elections in April 2017 under the new constitutional arrangements
saw the Republican Party maintain power. OSCE noted some improvements but
significant issues remained including vote buying. Institutions functioned
in constrained mode. Growing civil society pressure on government.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Armenia, 2017. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2018.assessment:
The Velvet Revolution (April-May 2018) transformed Armenia's institutional functioning.
Sargsyan resigned as prime minister after just 3 days; Pashinyan was elected. Snap
parliamentary elections in December 2018 produced a competitive outcome with
Pashinyan's My Step coalition winning 70% legitimately. Judiciary independence
improved; anti-corruption prosecution of former officials began.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Armenia, 2018. Coppedge et al. (2024)
OSCE/ODIHR, Final Report on December 2018 Parliamentary Elections

2019.assessment:
Institutions continued to improve under Pashinyan. Anti-corruption drive led to
prosecution of former presidents Kocharian and Sargsyan. Parliament was active
and representative. Judicial reform was underway but incomplete. Civil society
was energized and engaged. Some concerns about "victor's justice" in prosecutions.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Armenia, 2019. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2020.assessment:
The 2020 Nagorno-Karabakh War severely tested Armenia's institutions. Martial law
was declared during the war. Post-war political crisis included opposition demands
for Pashinyan's resignation, military leadership tensions, and street protests.
Democratic institutions survived but were severely stressed.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Armenia, 2020. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2021.assessment:
Snap parliamentary elections in June 2021 saw Pashinyan win re-election with
approximately 54% of the vote, confirming democratic mandate. OSCE/ODIHR assessed
elections positively overall. Institutions functioned through the political crisis.
Constitutional Court faced government pressure over composition disputes.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Armenia, 2021. Coppedge et al. (2024)
OSCE/ODIHR, Final Report on June 2021 Parliamentary Elections

2022.assessment:
Institutions remained functional under pressure. The September 2022 Azerbaijani
offensive created a national security crisis managed through democratic institutions.
Armenia applied for EU candidate status (informally). Judicial reform continued.
Constitutional Court reform was ongoing. Parliament was active and plural.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Armenia, 2022. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2023.assessment:
Institutions continued to improve. The September 2023 NK offensive and subsequent
mass refugee influx were managed through state institutions. Armenia signed agreements
with the EU and participated actively in international organizations. Judiciary reform
advanced. Media freedom was respected.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Armenia, 2023. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2024.assessment:
Armenia maintained functional democratic institutions. The EU partnership agreement
was advancing; judicial reform continued; elections were held with improved standards.
The peace treaty process with Azerbaijan reduced the security crisis context that
had strained institutions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025, Armenia

2025.assessment:
Armenia continued on its democratic institutional improvement trajectory.
EU integration pathway supported continued institutional reform.

sources.citation: News reports, Armenia democratic trajectory, 2025

2005.assessment:
Political violence in Armenia was minimal in 2005. The frozen Nagorno-Karabakh
conflict produced sporadic ceasefire violations and sniper deaths along the Line
of Contact, accounting for most conflict deaths. Domestic political violence was
low, with no significant armed insurgency or state repression campaigns.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Armenia, 2005

2006.assessment:
Minimal political violence in Armenia proper. Ongoing low-level ceasefire violations
along the NK Line of Contact continued to produce small numbers of deaths annually.
No major domestic armed conflict or significant state repression.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Armenia, 2006

2007.assessment:
Low-level political violence persisted through ceasefire violations along the NK
Line of Contact. Armenia's domestic political scene was tense as elections approached
but not yet violent. No significant armed insurgency or terrorism.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Armenia, 2007

2008.assessment:
Post-election violence in March 2008 killed approximately 10 protesters and police
when security forces dispersed demonstrations against disputed presidential election
results that brought Serzh Sargsyan to power. This was the worst domestic political
violence in Armenia in years, combined with ongoing NK ceasefire violations.

sources.citation: HRW, 'Armenia: Investigate Post-Election Violence', 2008
UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Armenia, 2008

2009.assessment:
Political violence returned to low levels after the 2008 post-election events.
NK ceasefire violations continued to produce deaths along the Line of Contact.
No significant domestic violence; state of emergency declared in March 2008 lifted.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Armenia, 2009

2010.assessment:
Minimal political violence in Armenia proper. NK ceasefire violations produced
the majority of conflict deaths. No significant domestic armed conflict or
organized political violence campaign against civilians.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Armenia, 2010

2011.assessment:
Low-level violence from NK ceasefire violations continued. Armenia proper was
largely peaceful. Post-Arab Spring context did not generate comparable political
mobilization in Armenia, though protests over local issues did occur.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Armenia, 2011

2012.assessment:
Violence remained minimal in Armenia proper. NK ceasefire violations continued
at a low level. Parliamentary elections in May 2012 saw some post-election tensions
but no significant violence.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Armenia, 2012

2013.assessment:
Low-level NK ceasefire violations continued. Armenia proper was relatively peaceful.
Presidential elections in February 2013 produced some protests but no significant
violence. Sargsyan re-elected in first round.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Armenia, 2013

2014.assessment:
Ceasefire violations along the NK Line of Contact intensified significantly in 2014
as Azerbaijan increased military activity. Deaths from ceasefire violations rose.
Armenia proper was peaceful. The escalation foreshadowed the April 2016 war.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Armenia, 2014

2015.assessment:
NK ceasefire violations continued at elevated levels in 2015. The Electric Yerevan
protests (June 2015) saw clashes with police but limited injuries. The transition to
a parliamentary system was approved in December 2015 referendum amid opposition protests.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Armenia, 2015
Reuters, 'Armenia Electric Yerevan Protests', June 2015

2016.assessment:
The April 2016 "Four-Day War" in Nagorno-Karabakh resulted in approximately 300-400
combat deaths (both sides). This was the most significant military escalation since
the 1994 ceasefire. Azerbaijan made small territorial gains before a ceasefire was
brokered. Additionally, in July 2016 an armed group seized a Yerevan police station
killing 2 officers and holding hostages for two weeks.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Armenia/NK, 2016
ICG, 'Nagorno-Karabakh: New Violence, Old Patterns', May 2016

2017.assessment:
Violence declined significantly after the April 2016 escalation. NK ceasefire
violations returned to lower levels. Armenia proper was peaceful with no significant
domestic political violence. Pre-Velvet Revolution political tensions were building
but not yet violent.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Armenia, 2017

2018.assessment:
The Velvet Revolution (April-May 2018) was remarkably peaceful, with no deaths
during the mass protests. NK ceasefire violations continued at a low level.
The transition of power was nonviolent despite the mass mobilization, reflecting
the nature of Armenia's peaceful political revolution.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Armenia, 2018
Carnegie Endowment, Armenia's Velvet Revolution, 2018

2019.assessment:
Armenia remained peaceful with minimal political violence. NK ceasefire violations
continued at a baseline level producing periodic deaths. No significant domestic
unrest or armed conflict. Post-Velvet Revolution political consolidation was peaceful.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Armenia, 2019

2020.assessment:
The 2020 Nagorno-Karabakh War (September 27 - November 9, 2020) was catastrophic
for Armenia. Estimates of total deaths range from 5,000-7,000 with Armenian/NKR
losses at roughly 3,800 killed (official Armenian figures) and Azerbaijani losses
of approximately 2,800 (official). The war ended with the November 10 ceasefire
that resulted in major Armenian territorial losses.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Armenia/NK, 2020
ICG Report: 'Averting a New Confrontation in Nagorno-Karabakh', November 2020

2021.assessment:
Violence declined significantly after the 2020 war ceasefire but did not end entirely.
The May 2021 Azerbaijani incursion into Armenian sovereign territory in Syunik and
Gegharkunik produced deaths and Armenian captures. Sporadic skirmishes along the new
border continued throughout the year.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Armenia, 2021
Reuters, 'Armenia says Azerbaijani forces crossed border', May 2021

2022.assessment:
The September 2022 Azerbaijani offensive into Armenian sovereign territory resulted
in approximately 200 Armenian soldiers killed over several days of fighting before a
ceasefire was brokered. This was an attack on internationally recognized Armenian
territory, not Nagorno-Karabakh. The offensive caused major political shock in Armenia.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Armenia, 2022
Reuters, 'Armenia, Azerbaijan agree ceasefire after new fighting', September 2022

2023.assessment:
The September 2023 Azerbaijani offensive in Nagorno-Karabakh lasted approximately
24 hours, with Armenian/NKR forces surrendering. Deaths were reported to be in the
low hundreds. The offensive precipitated the complete depopulation of Nagorno-Karabakh
as ~100,000 Armenians fled to Armenia. Armenia proper was not directly attacked.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, NK/Azerbaijan, 2023
BBC News, 'Nagorno-Karabakh: Azeri offensive ends Armenian self-rule', September 2023

2024.assessment:
Armenia was largely at peace in 2024 following the resolution of the NK conflict.
Peace treaty negotiations with Azerbaijan continued. Sporadic border incidents
occurred but no significant armed conflict. Armenia proper was stable.

sources.citation: ACLED Data for Armenia, 2024

2025.assessment:
Armenia continued peace negotiations with Azerbaijan. No significant conflict
deaths reported. Armenia proper was stable with ongoing border demarcation
as the primary security concern.

sources.citation: News reports, Armenia-Azerbaijan peace process, 2025

2005.assessment:
Armenia proper was fully under central government control. Armenian-backed forces
controlled Nagorno-Karabakh and seven surrounding Azerbaijani districts (~14% of
Azerbaijan's territory) since the 1994 ceasefire. The frozen conflict remained stable
with periodic ceasefire violations along the Line of Contact.

sources.citation: OSCE Minsk Group, Status Reports, 2005

2006.assessment:
The frozen conflict in Nagorno-Karabakh remained stable. Armenia proper was under
full central government control. OSCE-mediated peace talks continued without
breakthrough. Ceasefire violations remained regular but no major escalation.

sources.citation: OSCE Minsk Group, Status Reports, 2006
OSCE Minsk Group, Madrid Principles, November 2007

2008.assessment:
The frozen conflict remained stable in 2008. Armenia proper was under full central
government control, though the March 2008 post-election violence in Yerevan (10 deaths)
demonstrated internal political tensions but did not affect territorial control.
The NK and surrounding territories remained under Armenian/NKR control.

sources.citation: HRW Report, Armenia: Lethal Force Against Protesters, March 2008

2009.assessment:
Frozen conflict status unchanged. Armenia proper under full central government control.
Armenian-Turkish normalization protocols were signed in October 2009 but never ratified,
reflecting diplomatic context. NK status quo continued.

sources.citation: OSCE Minsk Group, Status Reports, 2009

2010.assessment:
Frozen conflict continued with no change. Armenia proper under full government control.
Line of Contact saw increasing ceasefire violations as negotiations stalled. NK and
seven surrounding districts remained under Armenian/NKR de facto control.

sources.citation: OSCE Minsk Group, Status Reports, 2010

2011.assessment:
Armenia proper under full central government control. The frozen NK conflict persisted
with no resolution. The Kazan summit between Armenian and Azerbaijani presidents in
June 2011 failed to produce a peace agreement on the Basic Principles document.

sources.citation: ICG, 'Armenia and Azerbaijan: Preventing War', February 2011

2012.assessment:
The NK frozen conflict remained stable with no major changes. Armenia proper under
full government control. Ceasefire violations along the Line of Contact continued.
No significant change in territorial control from prior year.

sources.citation: OSCE Minsk Group, Status Reports, 2012

2013.assessment:
Frozen conflict status unchanged. Armenia proper under full government control.
Ceasefire violations continued along the Line of Contact. No significant diplomatic
progress toward resolution.

sources.citation: OSCE Minsk Group, Status Reports, 2013

2014.assessment:
The frozen conflict continued with escalating ceasefire violations. Armenia proper
under full central government control. The Line of Contact saw more intense skirmishes
as Azerbaijan increased military spending and rhetoric. No territorial changes occurred.

sources.citation: ICG, 'Nagorno-Karabakh: Viewing the Conflict from the Ground', 2014

2015.assessment:
Frozen conflict persisted with continued ceasefire violations, reaching their highest
level in years. Armenia proper was fully under central government control. NK and
surrounding territories remained under Armenian/NKR control with no territorial changes.

sources.citation: OSCE Minsk Group, Status Reports, 2015

2016.assessment:
Armenia controlled its own internationally recognized territory but did not control
Nagorno-Karabakh (de facto controlled by Armenian forces) or the surrounding occupied
territories. The April 2016 "Four-Day War" saw brief Azerbaijani gains, signaling
vulnerability of the frozen conflict status quo but the front line returned largely
to pre-war positions.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Nagorno-Karabakh: New Violence, Old Patterns, May 2016

2017.assessment:
The situation remained largely frozen following the April 2016 war. Armenian forces
maintained control of Nagorno-Karabakh and surrounding territories while the Line of
Contact remained volatile with frequent ceasefire violations. Armenia proper was fully
under central government control.

sources.citation: OSCE Minsk Group, Status Reports, 2017

2018.assessment:
Following the Velvet Revolution of April-May 2018, Armenia's new government under
Nikol Pashinyan inherited the Nagorno-Karabakh frozen conflict. Armenia proper was
fully under central government control with no significant domestic territorial
challenges. The NK conflict remained frozen along the same lines.

sources.citation: Carnegie Endowment, 'Armenia's Velvet Revolution', 2018

2019.assessment:
Armenia proper remained under full central government control. The Nagorno-Karabakh
frozen conflict persisted with Armenian/NKR forces holding the region and adjacent
territories. Ceasefire violations continued but no major escalation occurred.
Pashinyan government maintained same territorial control as previous governments.

sources.citation: OSCE Minsk Group Status Reports, 2019

2020.assessment:
The September-November 2020 Nagorno-Karabakh War fundamentally altered territorial
control. Azerbaijan recaptured approximately 75% of the previously Armenian/NKR-held
territories during the 44-day war. The November 2020 ceasefire agreement mediated by
Russia required Armenian forces to withdraw from all surrounding districts and left
only the core Nagorno-Karabakh region under NKR control with Russian peacekeepers.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Averting a New Confrontation in Nagorno-Karabakh, November 2020
Reuters, 'Armenia and Azerbaijan sign deal to end Nagorno-Karabakh war', 10 November 2020

2021.assessment:
Following the 2020 war, Armenia proper remained under full central government control,
but the remaining Nagorno-Karabakh core was now under Russian peacekeeping with NKR
authorities. Border demarcation tensions with Azerbaijan arose, with Azerbaijani forces
entering Armenian territory in Syunik and Gegharkunik provinces in May 2021,
complicating Armenia's own territorial integrity.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Azerbaijan and Armenia: The Great Gamble Pays Off, December 2020
Reuters, 'Armenia accuses Azerbaijan of border incursion', May 2021

2022.assessment:
In September 2022, Azerbaijan launched a military offensive into Armenian sovereign
territory, occupying portions of Syunik and other border regions. Though most territory
was recovered or held under ceasefire, this represented direct threat to Armenia proper.
Nagorno-Karabakh remained under Russian peacekeeping with NKR authority increasingly
under pressure.

sources.citation: ICG Report: After the Azerbaijani Offensive, October 2022

2023.assessment:
In September 2023, Azerbaijan launched a lightning offensive against the remaining
Nagorno-Karabakh region, ending NKR authority within 24 hours. Virtually the entire
Armenian/NKR population (~100,000 people) fled to Armenia. Armenia proper remained
under full central government control with no more NK-related territorial issues,
though border demarcation with Azerbaijan remained contentious.

sources.citation: ICG Report: Nagorno-Karabakh: A Dangerous New Chapter, September 2023
BBC News, 'Nagorno-Karabakh: Azeri offensive ends Armenian self-rule', September 2023

2024.assessment:
Armenia proper was under full central government control. The Nagorno-Karabakh issue
was resolved (from a territorial perspective) by the September 2023 Azerbaijani
offensive. Peace treaty negotiations between Armenia and Azerbaijan continued throughout
2024, with border demarcation commissions working on disputed sections of the frontier.

sources.citation: Reuters, 'Armenia-Azerbaijan peace talks progress', 2024

2025.assessment:
Armenia maintained full control of its internationally recognized territory. A peace
treaty with Azerbaijan was still under negotiation but Armenia proper was stable.
Some border villages remained subject to demarcation disputes but no significant
contested territory remained.

sources.citation: OSCE, Armenia-Azerbaijan peace process updates, 2025

2005.assessment:
Armenia was not included in the 2006 Open Budget Survey round (first survey was 2006,
results published late 2006). No OBI score available for 2005. Budget processes
were opaque under Kocharian government; limited public access to fiscal documents.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2006

2006.assessment:
Armenia not included in the inaugural 2006 Open Budget Survey. Budget transparency
was limited; the government published some budget documents but access was restricted.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2006

2007.assessment:
No OBI data for 2007 (OBS is biennial; 2008 survey covers this period).
Budget transparency remained limited under Kocharian administration.

2008.assessment:
Armenia received an OBI score of 44 in the 2008 Open Budget Survey, placing it in
the "limited information" category. The score reflected partial publication of
budget documents — some were available but not all required documents were
accessible to the public. The post-election crisis in March 2008 did not
materially affect budget document publication.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2008

2009.assessment:
Inter-survey year; value estimated from OBS 2008 score of 44. Budget transparency
remained at similar levels with partial publication of fiscal documents.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2008 (interpolation basis)

2010.assessment:
Armenia maintained an OBI score of approximately 44 in the 2010 Open Budget Survey.
Budget documents were partially published. The government made limited improvements
to fiscal transparency during this period.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2010

2012.assessment:
Armenia's OBI score improved slightly to approximately 47 in the 2012 Open Budget
Survey, indicating modest improvement in budget document publication and accessibility.
Still in the "limited information" category but trending toward "some information."

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2012

2015.assessment:
Armenia achieved an OBI score of approximately 51 in the 2015 Open Budget Survey,
crossing into the "some information" category (41-60). This reflected continued
improvement in budget document publication, including more accessible budget
proposals and audit reports.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2015

2017.assessment:
Armenia's OBI score improved to approximately 54 in the 2017 Open Budget Survey,
continuing the upward trend. Budget documents were increasingly published online
and accessible to the public. Pre-revolution Armenia showed improving fiscal
transparency.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2017

2018.assessment:
Post-Velvet Revolution, the new Pashinyan government pledged significantly improved
transparency. Budget document publication improved rapidly in 2018-2019.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2019 (basis for interpolation)

2019.assessment:
Armenia's OBI score reached approximately 60 in the 2019 Open Budget Survey,
reflecting significant improvements following the 2018 Velvet Revolution. The
new Pashinyan government published comprehensive budget documents and improved
public engagement with fiscal processes.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2019

2020.assessment: Inter-survey year; interpolated value. NK war (2020) did not materially affect budget publication.

2021.assessment:
Armenia scored 61 in the 2021 Open Budget Survey transparency component, ranking 33rd
among 120 countries assessed. This score indicates the country publishes enough
material to support informed public debate. Public participation score was only
6/100, significantly below the global average.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2021 Armenia

2023.assessment:
Armenia scored 60 in the 2023 Open Budget Survey transparency component, near its
2021 score of 61. This slight decline was negligible and Armenia remained in the
"some information" category. Budget oversight score was 59/100. Public participation
remained very low at 11/100.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2023 Armenia

2024.assessment:
No new OBS survey in 2024. Score estimated to hold stable at ~60 based on 2023 result.
Armenia continued EU integration engagement, supporting fiscal transparency.

2025.assessment:
OBS 2025 not yet published. Score estimated to remain at ~60-62 based on recent trend
and continued EU integration which incentivizes fiscal transparency improvements.

2005.assessment:
WJP Rule of Law Index did not cover Armenia in this period (first published 2008,
broader country coverage added progressively). Legal transparency was low under
Kocharian; laws were not reliably published or accessible; judiciary lacked independence.

sources.citation: Freedom House Nations in Transit 2005, Armenia

2007.assessment:
WJP data not available for Armenia in 2007. Limited legal transparency under
Kocharian/Sargsyan era; selective enforcement of laws and limited public access
to government information.

2008.assessment:
WJP Rule of Law Index first published in 2008 but did not initially include Armenia.
The post-election crisis in March 2008 severely damaged legal transparency —
a state of emergency suspended normal legal processes; independent oversight
of government action was curtailed.

sources.citation: HRW Armenia post-election crackdown 2008

2009.assessment:
WJP data not available for Armenia. Limited legal transparency continued after
the 2008 crisis. Some recovery as state of emergency was lifted.

2010.assessment:
WJP data not available for Armenia in 2010. Legal transparency remained constrained.
Laws were published in official gazette but public access to government information
was limited. Selective enforcement of laws continued.

2011.assessment: WJP not available for Armenia. Limited legal transparency continued.

2012.assessment:
WJP not available for Armenia in 2012. Legal transparency remained limited.
Government information publication was improving gradually but still opaque
by international standards.

2013.assessment:
WJP data not available for Armenia. Limited legal transparency; government
information not systematically published in accessible formats.

2014.assessment:
WJP data not available for Armenia in 2014. Armenia adopted constitutional amendments
moving to a parliamentary system; legal framework was changing but transparency
remained limited.

2015.assessment:
WJP Rule of Law Index began covering Armenia in approximately 2015. Armenia's
Factor 3 (Open Government) score was estimated at approximately 0.38 (38/100),
reflecting limited government transparency — laws were sometimes not widely
publicized; right to information was restricted; civic participation in governance
was constrained.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index, Armenia profile
Freedom House Nations in Transit 2015, Armenia

2016.assessment:
Armenia's legal transparency remained limited. WJP Factor 3 score estimated at
approximately 0.38. Constitutional referendum in 2015 (moving to parliamentary
system) did not immediately improve legal transparency.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index

2017.assessment:
Armenia's legal transparency estimated at WJP Factor 3 ~0.39 in 2017. Limited
open government; laws not fully accessible; limited right to information implementation.
Pre-revolution: Sargsyan government had limited fiscal and legal transparency.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2017-2018

2018.assessment:
Following the 2018 Velvet Revolution, Armenia's legal transparency began improving.
The new Pashinyan government opened government data, improved the right to information
regime, and began publishing more government information online. WJP Factor 3
estimated at approximately 0.43 for 2018-2019 period.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2019

2019.assessment:
Armenia joined the Open Government Partnership in 2019, reflecting improved
commitment to legal transparency. WJP Factor 3 estimated at approximately 0.44,
showing improvement from pre-revolution levels. Government information portal
expanded. Right to information improved.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2019-2020
Open Government Partnership Armenia

2020.assessment:
Armenia's legal transparency remained at improved post-revolution levels.
NK war (2020) declared martial law but did not fundamentally alter the legal
transparency framework. WJP Factor 3 estimated at approximately 0.44.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2021

2021.assessment:
Armenia's legal transparency estimated at WJP Factor 3 ~0.45 in 2021. Continued
improvement in government information publication. Armenia's OGP action plan
commitments were being implemented. Some concerns about post-war government
restrictions but overall improved from pre-revolution baseline.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2021-22
OGP Armenia Results Report 2020-2022

2022.assessment:
Armenia's legal transparency continued gradual improvement. WJP Factor 3 estimated
at approximately 0.46. The September 2022 Azerbaijani offensive and subsequent
political tensions did not fundamentally affect legal transparency measures.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2022

2023.assessment:
Armenia's legal transparency continued improving trajectory. WJP Factor 3 estimated
at approximately 0.47 for 2023. EU partnership trajectory and OGP commitments
supported continued improvements to government information publication and right
to information implementation.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2023
OGP Armenia Results Report 2022-2024

2024.assessment:
Armenia's legal transparency estimated at WJP Factor 3 ~0.47. EU comprehensive
partnership and OGP membership continued to drive improvements. Armenia ranked
improving in several governance indices for this period.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2024

2025.assessment:
WJP 2025 data not yet published. Legal transparency expected to continue
gradual improvement trajectory driven by EU partnership and OGP commitments.

2005.assessment:
Press freedom in Armenia was restricted in 2005. Broadcast media was dominated
by pro-government outlets. The major independent TV station A1+ had its broadcast
license revoked in 2002 and had not been restored despite court rulings. Print
media had more diversity. Armenian journalists faced pressure and harassment.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2005
Freedom House, Press Freedom Report Armenia, 2005

2006.assessment:
Press freedom remained constrained. TV broadcasters faced licensing pressure.
Online media was growing as an alternative. Print press had more independence.
RSF ranked Armenia in the lower half of the index.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2006

2007.assessment:
Press freedom situation remained difficult pre-election. TV broadcasters remained
under government pressure. Online media growing. Some independent print outlets.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2007

2008.assessment:
Press freedom sharply deteriorated following the March 2008 post-election
crackdown. A state of emergency was declared that included severe media
restrictions — broadcasts from independent outlets were blocked; journalists
were detained; social media access was disrupted. This was Armenia's worst
press freedom crisis post-independence.

sources.citation: RSF, Armenia press freedom crisis March 2008
HRW, Armenia post-election media restrictions, 2008

2009.assessment:
Press freedom partially recovered as state of emergency was lifted. Broadcast
media still heavily controlled. Online media growing. Armenian journalists
faced continuing harassment. RSF ranked Armenia as having difficult press
freedom environment.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2009

2010.assessment:
Difficult press freedom environment continued. Broadcast media dominated by
pro-government outlets. Online media was becoming more significant. A1+ TV
station still not restored despite years of legal battles.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2010

2011.assessment:
Press freedom remained difficult. Social media and online news became more
important as alternatives to controlled broadcast media. Government pressure
on critical journalists continued.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2011

2012.assessment:
Difficult press environment. Parliamentary elections in May 2012 occurred without
independent election media coverage. Broadcast media continued to be dominated by
pro-government outlets.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2012

2013.assessment:
Press freedom remained difficult. Online media grew in importance. Government
maintained control over most broadcast media. Armenia ranked around 80-100th
in RSF index in this period.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2013

2014.assessment:
Press freedom situation remained difficult with government-controlled broadcast
media. Online media and social media continued to provide alternative voices.
Self-censorship was common among journalists.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2014

2015.assessment:
The Electric Yerevan protests were organized largely via social media, demonstrating
the importance of online media. Press freedom remained difficult for broadcast.
Constitutional referendum coverage was pro-government. RSF rank approximately 78th.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2015

2016.assessment:
Press freedom remained difficult. Online media was growing but broadcast continued
under government influence. The April NK war coverage was controlled.
RSF ranked Armenia approximately 70-80th.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2016

2017.assessment:
Press freedom remained difficult. Pre-revolution civic media and social media
outlets were growing. Nikol Pashinyan's Haykakan Zhamanak newspaper was
an example of critical independent print media. Broadcast still controlled.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2017

2018.assessment:
Press freedom improved significantly following the Velvet Revolution. RSF ranked
Armenia 79th in 2018 (improving to 61st in 2019). Post-revolution, oligarchic
control of media was broken; several major outlets changed ownership; broadcast
media became more pluralistic. Pashinyan was a former journalist himself.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2018

2019.assessment:
Press freedom significantly improved. RSF ranked Armenia 61st in 2019 — a major
jump. Major positive changes: oligarchic owners replaced; more diverse TV ownership;
editorial independence increased. Some concerns remained about government influence
on media companies with state advertising.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2019

2020.assessment:
Press freedom maintained improvement. RSF ranked Armenia around 61st. During the
NK war (September-November 2020), martial law included some media restrictions but
the overall environment was substantially better than pre-revolution.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2020

2021.assessment:
Press freedom remained improved. RSF maintained Armenia in the 60-70 range.
Post-war political polarization created some partisan media pressure. But overall
environment significantly better than pre-2018.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2021

2022.assessment:
RSF introduced a new methodology in 2022. Armenia received a score of 53.38
(ranked 51st globally) under the new methodology — reflecting significant
press freedom improvements post-revolution. The September 2022 Azerbaijani
offensive did not significantly affect domestic press freedom.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2022

2023.assessment:
Armenia received an RSF score of 61.13 in 2023, ranking approximately 43rd
globally. This represented continued improvement. Media pluralism had significantly
improved since 2018. The NK crisis coverage was free from major government restrictions.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2023

2024.assessment:
Armenia continued to improve in RSF rankings, reflecting continued media
pluralization and reduced government interference. EU integration trajectory
supported continued press freedom improvements.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2024

2025.assessment:
Press freedom expected to remain improved. EU partnership expected to further
support media independence.

2005.assessment:
Armenia had strong statistical capacity by regional standards in 2005, scoring 84.44
on the World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator. The National Statistical Service
of Armenia (ArmStat) had been reformed after independence and was producing
reasonably comprehensive national accounts, demographic, and social statistics.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Armenia

2006.assessment:
Armenia's statistical capacity improved to 86.67 in 2006. ArmStat continued
producing national statistics aligned with international standards. Good coverage
across economic, demographic, and social statistics.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Armenia

2007.assessment:
Armenia's statistical capacity improved significantly to 90.00 in 2007. ArmStat
continued improving data collection, methodology, and publication standards.
Strong performance across methodology, source data, and periodicity dimensions.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Armenia

2008.assessment:
Armenia's statistical capacity reached 92.22 in 2008. Strong performance reflected
continued improvements to ArmStat's operations. The post-election crisis in March 2008
did not materially affect statistical operations.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Armenia

2009.assessment:
Armenia's statistical capacity peaked at 93.33 in 2009. Despite the economic crisis
(GDP fell sharply in 2009), statistical operations remained strong. ArmStat continued
publishing timely and comprehensive statistics.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Armenia

2010.assessment:
Armenia's statistical capacity remained high at 92.22 in 2010. ArmStat continued
publishing comprehensive statistics aligned with international standards.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Armenia

2011.assessment:
Armenia maintained statistical capacity at 92.22 in 2011. Consistent performance
across data collection, methodology, and publication.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Armenia

2012.assessment:
Armenia's statistical capacity declined to 86.67 in 2012, a notable drop from
previous years. This may reflect methodological reassessment by the World Bank
or specific gaps in data publication identified during the assessment period.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Armenia

2013.assessment:
Armenia's statistical capacity partially recovered to 88.89 in 2013. Improvements
in specific dimensions of the WB SCI assessment.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Armenia

2014.assessment:
Armenia's statistical capacity was 87.78 in 2014. Reasonably strong but slightly
below peak years. ArmStat continued publishing regular statistics.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Armenia

2015.assessment:
Armenia's statistical capacity returned to peak levels at 93.33 in 2015. Strong
performance in data methodology, source data quality, and periodicity of publication.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Armenia

2016.assessment:
Armenia's statistical capacity remained strong at 92.22 in 2016. ArmStat
continued to produce comprehensive and timely statistics.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Armenia

2017.assessment:
Armenia's statistical capacity was 91.11 in 2017, marginally below previous peak.
Strong statistical system continuing to support IMF/WB data provision requirements.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Armenia

2018.assessment:
Armenia's statistical capacity rose to 93.33 in 2018, coinciding with the Velvet
Revolution. The new government was committed to transparency; ArmStat operations
remained strong and independent.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Armenia

2019.assessment:
Armenia maintained statistical capacity at 93.33 in 2019. ArmStat continued
strong performance. Post-revolution transparency agenda supported statistical
publication improvements.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Armenia

2020.assessment:
Armenia achieved its highest recorded statistical capacity score of 94.44 in 2020,
the last year the World Bank published SCI data before discontinuing the indicator.
This reflects excellent performance in data methodology, source data, and periodicity
dimensions. NK war did not affect statistical publication.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Armenia

2021.assessment:
World Bank discontinued the Statistical Capacity Indicator after 2020. Armenia's
statistical capacity estimated to remain at approximately the 2020 level (94.44)
based on ArmStat continuing strong operations and EU data harmonization efforts.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator 2020 (final year)

2022.assessment:
Estimated continued strong statistical capacity. ArmStat published regular
statistical yearbooks and online data. EU harmonization continues.

2023.assessment:
Estimated continued strong statistical capacity. Armenia's EU partnership
trajectory supporting data harmonization with EU standards.

2024.assessment: Estimated continued strong statistical capacity based on 2020 trajectory.
